answers.field_label: How You Heard And Why Success Strategy Session
answers.value:
I first heard about your coaching services through a friend from my coworking space, Jenna Morales, who worked with you last year. She knew I’d been complaining (a lot) about my home office getting out of control and how it was starting to affect my business. She forwarded me one of your newsletters and a link to your website, and I ended up watching a couple of your short videos on clutter and productivity. I also saw your name mentioned in a local San Diego moms’ Facebook group where several people recommended you specifically for helping with home offices and paper clutter.

The short version is: my physical environments have gotten to a point where they’re clearly holding me back, and I feel like I’ve hit my own limit on trying to fix it by myself.

Longer version:

I’ve been working primarily from home for the last four years, doing freelance graphic design plus a part-time marketing role for a small eco-friendly skincare company. I started out pretty organized, but over time my office, dining room, and even my bedroom have turned into a mix of work materials, kids’ school papers (I have two kids, ages 8 and 11), unopened mail, half-finished craft projects, and random things that don’t have a home. I keep thinking I’ll take one weekend to “just deal with it,” but I end up moving piles from one surface to another and never really finishing.

I want a Success Strategy Session because I’m noticing specific ways this clutter and lack of systems are hurting me:

1. **Focus and productivity at work:**
   - I get distracted constantly by stacks of paper and random things in my line of sight. I’ll sit down to design a brochure, notice a pile of receipts I haven’t entered, start sorting them, then remember an email I never answered. I end days feeling busy but not actually productive.
   - I have a hard time finding client notes and sketches. There have been a couple of embarrassing moments where a client has asked a follow-up question and I’ve had to say, “Let me track that down,” because I couldn’t quickly find my original notes.

2. **Time management and procrastination:**
   - I underestimate how long tasks will take and overbook myself. I also procrastinate on things that require dealing with clutter, like filing paperwork or clearing out my email inbox, because it feels overwhelming and I don’t have a clear system.
   - I’ve paid two late fees in the last six months because bills got buried in piles of mail on the kitchen counter. That was a wake-up call that this isn’t just an aesthetic problem; it’s affecting my finances.

3. **Family stress and home environment:**
   - My husband is fairly tidy and gets frustrated with the constant “projects” spread out everywhere. We’ve had arguments where he feels like the house is never really relaxing because there’s always a mess in the background.
   - My kids have started saying things like, “I don’t know where this goes,” or “I’ll just put it on Mom’s desk,” which makes me realize I’m modeling disorganization for them.

4. **Mental load and guilt:**
   - I carry this low-level guilt all the time that I should be more on top of the house and my work systems. I’ll sit down to read or go for a walk and immediately think, “You should be sorting that box in the hallway instead.” It’s draining.
   - I’ve tried reading organizing blogs and watching YouTube videos, but I get overwhelmed by all the advice and end up doing nothing or starting something I don’t finish.

What I’m hoping to get from a Success Strategy Session with you:

- An outside, experienced perspective on what’s really going on with my clutter and time management—not just “I’m messy,” but what habits and patterns are driving this.
- Help identifying **one or two key areas** to focus on first (I suspect it’s my home office and paper management) instead of trying to overhaul everything at once and burning out.
- A realistic, step-by-step plan that fits my actual life as a working mom, not a fantasy schedule where I magically have six free hours every weekend.
- Concrete strategies for:
  - Managing incoming paper (mail, school forms, business receipts, client contracts).
  - Keeping my workspace clear enough so I can actually think.
  - Structuring my days so I’m not constantly reacting and switching tasks.
- Some accountability and encouragement. I tend to get excited at the beginning of a project, then lose momentum when things get tedious or emotional (like sorting sentimental items or old client files I feel guilty about).

Overall, I want to feel like the spaces I live and work in are supporting me instead of draining me. I’m not aiming for perfection or a Pinterest-perfect home; I just want to be able to find what I need quickly, follow through on my commitments, and feel calmer and more in control of my time and environment. I’m hoping this session will give me clarity, a starting point, and a few practical tools I can actually stick with long term.

persona.background_context:
This is Melissa, a 38-year-old working mom in San Diego who is constantly juggling client deadlines, school pickups, and the never-ending influx of paper that seems to land on every flat surface of her house. She and her husband Daniel bought their three-bedroom home in the Clairemont/Bay Ho area in 2016, just before prices jumped, and they’ve slowly been updating it room by room. The third bedroom became her home office when she went full-time freelance in 2020, shifting away from an in-house design role at a local agency. Now she splits her time between her own graphic design clients—mostly small businesses, yoga studios, and local non-profits—and a steady 20-hour-a-week marketing coordinator role for an eco-friendly skincare company called SeaBloom Organics, whose founder she met at a women-in-business networking event in 2021.

Most weekdays start early: she’s usually up around 6:15 a.m., getting coffee going and packing lunches while mentally running through her to-do list. Ethan has band practice twice a week before school, and Lily has soccer practice after school on Tuesdays and Thursdays, so there’s a constant shuffling of drop-offs and pickups between her and Daniel. After school drop-off, Melissa heads back home, clears a small space on her office desk amid the piles of sketches, receipts, and kids’ art projects, and tries to get into a focused work block. She might spend the morning refining a logo concept for a new client in North Park, then switch gears to schedule Instagram posts and write an email newsletter for SeaBloom. Her time management is hit-or-miss; some days she hits a groove and feels on top of everything, and other days she bounces between half-finished tasks, interrupted by the sight of an overflowing in-tray or an email reminder about a bill she meant to pay last week.

The house itself reflects this push-pull between intention and reality. The living room is cozy, with a gray sectional, plants by the window, and kids’ books tucked into baskets, but the dining table often disappears under school forms, art supplies, and product samples from SeaBloom. Her office—technically her dedicated workspace—has become a catch-all for anything that doesn’t have a clear home: Amazon returns, boxes of old client files, partially sorted tax documents, and a growing stack of unopened mail. She’s had a couple of recent wake-up calls: a late fee on the SDG&E bill in December because it got buried, and a mildly embarrassing moment in January when a long-term client asked about feedback they’d given on a brochure revision and she had to spend ten minutes digging through overlapping sketch pads and printed proofs to find her notes.

Family dynamics are generally loving but strained at times by the clutter and the mental load Melissa carries. Daniel is supportive and proud of her freelance career but gets frustrated when the kitchen counters are covered or when her “temporary project piles” linger for weeks. They’ve had a few tense Sunday evenings where he’s trying to reset the house for the week while she’s glued to her laptop finishing a client presentation she underestimated the time for. Ethan is starting middle school in the fall and is into video games and robotics; he’s asked for a clearer workspace at the dining table for his Lego and coding projects, which adds to Melissa’s sense that they need better systems. Lily is imaginative and loves crafts, which means glitter, beads, and construction paper seem to multiply everywhere. Melissa feels a pang when she hears her kids say things like, “I’ll just put it on Mom’s desk,” realizing they see her space as the default dumping ground.

In the last year, Melissa has been trying to make incremental changes rather than chasing an all-or-nothing reset. She joined a local coworking space in Pacific Beach one day a week in late 2023 to have at least one clutter-free workday, and she meets her friend Jenna there occasionally for a working lunch. She’s experimented with time-blocking on Google Calendar, setting aside Monday mornings for admin and invoicing, but the blocks often get derailed by urgent client requests or household tasks that feel impossible to ignore. For exercise and sanity, she walks around the neighborhood or down at Mission Bay a couple of times a week, often listening to podcasts about productivity and small business growth. She and Daniel have also started a low-key Friday pizza-and-movie night with the kids to carve out family time that isn’t about chores or homework.

As spring 2026 unfolds, Melissa is looking ahead at a few key goals: she wants to streamline her client onboarding process, finally set up a simple digital system for client notes and contracts, and tame the paper chaos that keeps tripping her up. Tax season was more stressful than it needed to be because receipts and 1099s were scattered in multiple piles and folders, and she’s determined not to repeat that next year. At the same time, she’s conscious of her energy and doesn’t want to burn out chasing some unrealistic image of perfection. What she wants most is a home and work setup that supports her: a clear desk most days, a reliable way to handle incoming mail and school forms, and a daily rhythm where she can end the day knowing what got done and what will wait until tomorrow—without the constant hum of guilt in the background.

question_secrets.question_text: How You Heard And Why Success Strategy Session

secrets.detail_content: I first heard about your Success Strategy Session last September (September 2024) when my friend from my coworking space, Jenna Morales, forwarded me your newsletter after I had a full-blown panic attack in the parking lot at the Hera Hub Coworking Space in Mission Valley because I’d just gotten off the phone with Holmes Elementary about Ethan’s missing assignments and another notice about his ADHD behavior plan review. I was crying in my car, surrounded by unopened mail and school forms, and texted Jenna that I felt like a terrible mom and a mess professionally. She replied later that afternoon with your newsletter and the link to your site, saying you’d helped her when she was going through postpartum depression and couldn’t keep up with her own paperwork.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer reveals a panic attack, a possible ADHD diagnosis/behavior plan for her child, and a friend’s postpartum depression—medical and mental health information about both herself and others, plus her child’s school issues. While it explains how she heard about the coach and why she’s seeking help, it goes far beyond what’s needed for a coaching intake form and exposes sensitive health and educational information that could impact how she and her family are perceived.

places: Hera Hub Coworking Space in Mission Valley
events: panic attack in the parking lot

appears_naturally_in.example: I’m still so grateful you sent me that Sue Crum newsletter last September after my meltdown in the Hera Hub parking lot when the school called about Ethan’s behavior plan again.

Subject: Why I finally booked that organizing coach

Remember when I had that panic attack in the Hera Hub lot after the call from Holmes about Ethan’s ADHD plan? That’s when Jenna sent me Sue’s info and said she’d helped her when she was dealing with postpartum depression and drowning in paperwork.

secrets.detail_content: I decided to book the Success Strategy Session in early January 2025 after a budgeting call with my parents, Pat and Bob Reynolds, at their house in Temecula, where we realized I’d racked up over $1,200 in late fees and overdraft charges in the last 12 months because I kept missing payment deadlines and misplacing bills in the piles around my home office and dining room. My mom actually pulled out a printout from our joint credit union account at Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union and highlighted every fee. That was humiliating enough that when I saw your name again in the San Diego Working Moms Facebook group that same week, I finally clicked through and booked the session, even though I’m nervous about spending more money.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail exposes specific financial struggles, including the exact amount of late fees and the existence of a joint account with her parents, which implies financial dependence and potential strain. While it clarifies why she’s motivated to seek help, the exact dollar amounts, joint account details, and emotional context of a budgeting intervention with her parents are more than a coach needs for an intake form and could affect how she’s judged financially.

artifacts.content:
Hi Hannah,

I finally have a quiet-ish minute while the kids are glued to Bluey and Daniel’s tinkering with the grill, so I wanted to actually say thank you for forwarding me that October newsletter from Sue last fall.

You were absolutely right that the “paper clutter costing you money” article could have been written about me. I still cringe when I think about March 2023 and that SeaBloom Organics project I had to cancel because I lost the signed contract and product brief somewhere in the black hole of my home office. Finding them three weeks after the deadline, buried under kids’ art and old client folders, was like a punch in the gut. That phone call at the La Jolla office with the owner, trying to explain why I’d dropped the ball, and then the 30% reduction in my monthly hours for six months… I don’t ever want to repeat that.

Between that and the late fees on bills and school forms getting lost in piles on the dining table, it finally pushed me to reach out to Sue. I’ve started blocking Monday mornings just for admin and paper triage. It’s not perfect, but it’s something. I even cleared enough off my desk that Ethan can sometimes use the corner for his Lego robotics stuff (when Lily isn’t “helping” with glitter).

How are things in Carlsbad? How’s work? If you’re free one weekend, maybe you could come down and help me decide what’s actually worth keeping from the kids’ artwork mountain. I trust your ruthless editor eye.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: That newsletter you sent last fall

artifacts.content:
Mom,

I’ve been meaning to follow up after our budgeting talk at the house in Temecula in early January. I’m still a little mortified thinking about you sitting at the kitchen table with that Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union printout, highlighter in hand, circling every single late fee and overdraft charge from the last 12 months. Seeing it added up—over $1,200 just because I kept misplacing bills in the piles around my office and the dining room—was a real wake-up call.

I know you and Dad weren’t trying to shame me, but it did sting. In a good way, I guess, because it finally pushed me to do something instead of just saying, “I’ll get organized when things calm down.” Spoiler: things never calm down.

That same week I saw Sue Crum’s name pop up again in the San Diego Working Moms Facebook group. I remembered Jenna talking about how much she helped when she was drowning in paperwork after the baby, so I booked a Success Strategy Session with her. I was nervous about spending more money right after staring at that $1,200 number, but it feels more like preventing future dumb fees.

We’ve already set up a simple system: one basket by the front door for all incoming mail, and I have “Money Monday” on my calendar to actually open, sort, and pay things. Daniel is cautiously optimistic.

Thank you (really) for sitting down with me and not just rolling your eyes at my mess. Tell Dad I’m working on it.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: About the money talk

artifacts.content:
Hey Jenna,

Catching up between Zoom calls and snack requests. I was thinking about you the other day driving past Hera Hub in Mission Valley and remembering that meltdown in the parking lot back in September 2024. I can still picture myself sitting in the car, surrounded by unopened mail and crumpled Holmes Elementary forms, sobbing after that call about Ethan’s missing assignments and yet another ADHD behavior plan review.

I felt like the worst mom and the most unprofessional “professional” ever. You texting me that afternoon and then forwarding Sue’s newsletter honestly changed the trajectory a bit. Knowing you’d worked with her when you were going through postpartum depression and drowning in your own paperwork made it feel less like a personal failure and more like… a solvable problem.

Fast forward: I finally booked with Sue after that brutal Temecula money talk with my parents in early January—turns out $1,200 in late fees will motivate a girl. She’s helping me build systems so the car doesn’t become a traveling file cabinet again.

Work-wise, SeaBloom is steady. I’m still doing about 20 hours a week for them plus my own clients. I’m trying to keep Fridays lighter so I can actually show up for pizza-and-movie night without my laptop. Ethan’s band practice and Lily’s soccer schedule are a whole second job, but you know how that goes.

Let me know when you’re next at Hera Hub PB. I’d love a coworking day that doesn’t end in a panic attack.

xo,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Remember the Hera Hub parking lot?

artifacts.content:
Hi Dr. Lewis,

I wanted to give you a little context before our next session because the clutter/time stuff has really come to a head over the last couple of years.

Back in June 2024, Daniel got a written warning from HR at SunWave Solar for missing a safety training. The whole thing started because he couldn’t find the printed schedule in our bedroom, which has morphed into a shared “office” with piles of my client folders and the kids’ school papers on the floor and dresser. He’d left the schedule on the dresser, it got buried under one of my “temporary” stacks, and he completely spaced the training. After the Zoom call with his HR manager, he was furious and scared, and we had a huge argument about the state of the room.

He ended up sitting at the dining table with his laptop, Googling “San Diego home office organizer” while I was still crying in the bedroom. That’s how he found Sue Crum’s website and emailed me the link with the subject line, “We need this before I lose my job over a piece of paper.” At the time I felt attacked, but looking back, he was legitimately worried.

Layer that on top of my own mess—like the SeaBloom contract I lost in March 2023 that led to the 30% hours cut for six months, and the $1,200 in late fees my parents pointed out in January—and it’s clear this isn’t just about being “a bit messy.”

I’d like to use our sessions to untangle why I keep recreating these chaos zones even when the consequences are so high.

Best,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Some context on the clutter/work stress

artifacts.content:
Digital journal – 2025-01-07

I’m writing this while the house is finally quiet. Today feels like a turning point, or at least I want it to.

In the last couple of years, the clutter hasn’t just been annoying; it’s been actively sabotaging us. March 2023 was the first big “oh no” moment: losing that signed SeaBloom Organics contract and product brief in my home office, only finding them three weeks after the deadline. That led to canceling the project, the awful phone call at the La Jolla office, and the 30% cut in my hours for six months. We tightened our belts and I told myself I’d get systems in place.

Then June 2024: Daniel’s written warning from HR at SunWave Solar for missing that safety training because the schedule was buried under piles in our bedroom/office. The fight that night was brutal, and when he Googled “San Diego home office organizer” at the dining table and sent me Sue’s link with “before I lose my job over a piece of paper” in the subject, I felt both attacked and seen.

September 2024: panic attack in the Hera Hub Mission Valley parking lot after the call from Holmes about Ethan’s missing assignments and another ADHD behavior plan review. Sitting in the car surrounded by unopened mail and school forms, texting Jenna that I felt like a terrible mom. She replied later with Sue’s newsletter.

And then early January 2025: Mom and Dad at the Temecula kitchen table, highlighting over $1,200 in late fees and overdrafts from our Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union account because I keep losing bills.

Today I finally booked a Success Strategy Session with Sue. I’m scared, embarrassed, hopeful. Mostly hopeful.

Event: Success Strategy Session with Sue Crum – home office + paper systems

Date: Wednesday, 2025-01-22
Time: 10:00 a.m. – 11:30 a.m.
Location: Zoom from home office (clear at least one visible surface!)
Attendees: Melissa Carpenter, Sue Crum

Notes / Prep:
- This has been a long time coming. Remember: this is the same coach Jenna worked with when she was dealing with postpartum depression and drowning in paperwork. Jenna first sent me Sue’s newsletter after my panic attack in the Hera Hub Mission Valley parking lot in September 2024, when Holmes Elementary called about Ethan’s missing assignments and yet another ADHD behavior plan review while I was literally sitting in a car full of unopened mail.

- Also think about the money angle: the budgeting talk at Mom and Dad’s place in Temecula in early January, when Mom pulled out the Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union statement and highlighted over $1,200 in late fees and overdraft charges from the last 12 months. That’s what finally pushed me to book this.

- Don’t forget Daniel’s perspective: the June 2024 blow-up in our bedroom after his Zoom with HR at SunWave Solar about the written warning for missing the safety training because the printed schedule was buried under my piles.

Questions for Sue:
• Where to start: home office vs. dining table?
• Simple system for incoming paper (mail, school forms, receipts).
• How to time-block realistically with client work + SeaBloom + kids’ schedules.

Hey Prof. Daniels,

I hope you’re well and that SDSU isn’t drowning you in committee meetings. I’ve been thinking about your old “systems save creativity” lecture a lot lately.

The last couple of years have been a mess, literally and figuratively. In March 2023, I lost a big SeaBloom Organics project because I misplaced the signed contract and product brief in my home office. I only found them three weeks after the deadline, buried under sketch pads and the kids’ artwork. That led to a really uncomfortable call at their La Jolla office and a 30% reduction in my monthly hours for six months. It shook my confidence more than I admitted at the time.

Then there was June 2024, when my husband Daniel (he’s a project manager at SunWave Solar) got a written warning from HR for missing a safety training because the printed schedule disappeared into the piles in our bedroom/office. We had a huge argument, and he ended up Googling “San Diego home office organizer” and emailing me a link to a coach with the subject line, “We need this before I lose my job over a piece of paper.”

On top of that, my parents sat me down in Temecula this January and walked me through a Temecula Valley Federal Credit Union statement showing over $1,200 in late fees and overdraft charges from the last year. All because bills and notices get buried.

I finally hired that organizer/coach, and we’re building basic systems: inbox trays, weekly admin blocks, digital backups for contracts. It’s humbling to admit I needed help with the stuff you warned us about back in 2008, but better late than never.

If you ever want a cautionary tale for your students, I’m your girl.

Best,
Melissa
